title: Dunder Mifflin Infinity
weakest_section: 31:00-33:00 range with standard client visit scenes

jokes.text: How dare you?
jokes.setup: Someone politely declines chips in normal conversation
jokes.punchline: Michael's dramatically outraged reaction to this minor social interaction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael Scott overreaction to nothing. Economy is excellent - just three words convey absurd indignation.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You don't want chips? What do you mean you don't want chips? Everybody wants chips. Chips are... chips are amazing. They're crispy, they're salty, they're... they're perfect. And you're just gonna sit there and say no? That's insane. That's crazy. You're crazy.

jokes.text: Is this about me and Jan in my office? Because I'll have you know that that was consensual... We're very discreet. And most people had left by that point.
jokes.setup: Toby mentions PDA complaints in general terms
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately assumes it's about him and overshares intimate details while claiming discretion
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael move - missing the point entirely while revealing too much. The contradiction between claiming discretion while being indiscreet is perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: We've been getting some complaints about PDA in the workplace.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. If this is about me and Holly, we are very professional. We barely touch at work.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I mean, sure, sometimes we'll hold hands under the conference table, or I'll brush up against her when we're filing, but that's it.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And anything that happens outside of work is between me and her. I'm not going to kiss and tell.

jokes.text: For the record, I've never been involved with anyone at work in any capacity.
jokes.setup: Michael just overshared about his workplace relationship
jokes.punchline: Toby's defensive clarification that he's never done what Michael just admitted to doing
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat showing Toby's awkwardness and need to distance himself from Michael's behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: I would never do that. I'm a professional.

jokes.text: Tuna!
jokes.setup: Jim and Pam reveal they're dating
jokes.punchline: Andy's enthusiastic reaction using Jim's fish nickname
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective character moment. Andy's enthusiasm and nickname use is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh my God! Jim and Pam?! That's... that's amazing! Halpert and Beesly, sitting in a tree! K-I-S-S-I-N-G! Oh man, Tuna's gonna be so happy!

jokes.text: Toby, was this your fun little way of congratulating us?
jokes.setup: Toby's memo inadvertently revealed Jim and Pam's relationship
jokes.punchline: Jim sarcastically suggesting Toby planned this as congratulations
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Jim's dry wit and ability to find humor in awkward situations.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What, you think Toby did this on purpose? Like, as a congratulations to us?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm sure he stayed up all night planning how to expose our relationship. That's so thoughtful of him.

jokes.text: Everyone, this is a day that will live in infamy... Because today... is the day... that Jim and Pam... become one. Actually, we're dating for 2 months.
jokes.setup: Michael builds up dramatic announcement about Jim and Pam 'becoming one'
jokes.punchline: Jim's matter-of-fact correction that they've been casually dating for months
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Michael's grandiose moment. The timing and contrast between dramatic buildup and mundane reality is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jim, Pam, I have an announcement to make. You two are becoming one. You're merging. It's a beautiful thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We've been dating for like three months.

jokes.text: Phone's ringing. No, no. Pam, let'em ring. Let the bells of Dunder-Mifflin chime out your love.
jokes.setup: Phone rings during Michael's celebration
jokes.punchline: Michael's poetic interpretation of office phone as 'bells chiming out love'
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael romanticism applied inappropriately. The phrase 'bells of Dunder-Mifflin chime out your love' is wonderfully absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's the phone.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Do you want me to get it?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, no. Listen to that. Those bells chiming out love. That's the sound of this office, Pam. That's the sound of family.

jokes.text: My heart soars... with the eagle's nest.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to express joy poetically
jokes.punchline: Mixing up 'eagle's nest' instead of proper idiom like 'eagle soaring'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael malapropism. The confidence with which he delivers nonsensical poetry is quintessential character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm not just happy, I'm like an eagle in its nest. Just soaring... nesting... I'm doing both at the same time.

jokes.text: Pam is the office mattress.
jokes.setup: Others celebrating Jim and Pam's relationship
jokes.punchline: Angela's cruel slut-shaming comment delivered in talking head
jokes.explanation: Perfectly in character for Angela's judgmental nature, though the joke itself is fairly basic mean humor.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's great that Jim and Pam found each other. Two people can bond over their shared lack of... standards.

jokes.text: Jim Halpert's off the market. Guess who just became the best looking single guy in the office?
jokes.setup: Kevin notes Jim is no longer single
jokes.punchline: Implies he's now the best looking single guy by process of elimination
jokes.explanation: Good self-deprecating humor that works because of Kevin's character and the logical absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Wait, Jim's not single anymore? That means I'm the best looking single guy here now.

jokes.text: Can you make that straighter? That's what she said.
jokes.setup: Jim asks innocent question about straightening something
jokes.punchline: Michael's immediate 'That's what she said' response
jokes.explanation: Standard execution of Michael's signature joke. Gets points for character consistency but lacks originality.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: How do you straighten that out?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's what she said.

jokes.text: How can you even use that one naturally? Blowing up balloons, I thought.
jokes.setup: Discussion about 'That's what she said' setups
jokes.punchline: Michael's oddly specific example of balloon blowing as natural setup
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about the running gag itself. Michael's weirdly innocent explanation adds character depth.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You see, Jim, a good 'That's what she said' joke needs the right setup. For instance, if someone says 'I'm having trouble blowing up this balloon,' that's a natural setup.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, that's... that's actually not that natural.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, no, think about it. You're at a birthday party, someone's struggling with a balloon, and boom — 'That's what she said.' It's perfect.

jokes.text: Pam and Jim are together. Ryan is visiting. Only thing that could make this day better is ice cream.
jokes.setup: Michael listing good things happening
jokes.punchline: Adding ice cream as equal priority to major life events
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment showing Michael's childlike priorities and joy in simple pleasures.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: My condo is gonna be awesome. I'm gonna have the best shower curtain, I'm gonna get the finest ice cream, the rarest ice cream, like... I'm gonna have a bidet. I'm gonna get the best condo.

jokes.text: It's a feral barn cat. I trapped him and I'm giving him to you as a replacement cat for the one I destroyed. Her name was Sprinkles. And his name is Garbage.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to make amends for killing Angela's cat
jokes.punchline: Offering a feral cat named 'Garbage' as replacement for beloved 'Sprinkles'
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd Dwight logic. The contrast between 'Sprinkles' and 'Garbage' is perfect, and the matter-of-fact delivery sells it completely.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Angela, I know I can never replace Sprinkles, but I found this cat and I think he could fill the void in your heart.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Dwight, what is that?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: His name is Garbage.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: He's a very good cat. He only bites when provoked.

jokes.text: Most calls him Garbage 'cause he likes to eat garbage. Don't you, Garbage?
jokes.setup: Explaining the cat's name
jokes.punchline: Affectionately addressing a feral cat that eats garbage
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the Garbage introduction. Dwight's tender tone with the disgusting cat is endearing and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I've named him Garbage. Because he eats garbage. And I love him.

jokes.text: He killed an entire family of raccoons. Look at him.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to sell Garbage as a useful cat
jokes.punchline: Proudly citing mass raccoon murder as a selling point
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dark Dwight humor. The casual mention of family murder as a positive trait is brilliantly twisted.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: This cat has killed more raccoons than any cat in the tri-county area. That's a selling point.

jokes.text: Well, I don't wanna speak for Jim, but it's, like, pretty official.
jokes.setup: Toby suggesting their relationship might be casual
jokes.punchline: Pam's understated correction while literally speaking for Jim
jokes.explanation: Gentle irony in Pam saying she won't speak for Jim while doing exactly that. Shows relationship confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: It's not casual. Jim and I are very serious.

jokes.text: Let's just wait and see what happens, you know. What? Well, let's just wait.
jokes.setup: Couple asking about official relationship paperwork
jokes.punchline: Toby's awkward suggestion to 'wait and see' about their relationship
jokes.explanation: Classic Toby awkwardness. His pessimistic assumption that relationships fail is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: Well, you'll need to fill out form 27-B for any officially recognized relationship status changes.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So we just fill this out and we're good?

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: Not exactly. I'd recommend you, uh, wait and see how things develop first. Relationships are unpredictable.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We've been together for years.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: Still. You never know. Better to be cautious.

jokes.text: He's back! And he's with a beard. He has facial hair. Look at him! All grown up and no place to go.
jokes.setup: Ryan returns to the office
jokes.punchline: Michael's overly excited commentary on Ryan's beard, ending with 'no place to go'
jokes.explanation: Michael's excited father-figure energy mixed with the accidentally insulting 'no place to go' is endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Ryan! Oh my God, look at you! You've got a beard now. That's... that's a bold choice. Very lumbersexual. Very... outdoorsy. You look like you're about to tell me about some artisanal coffee or take me on a spiritual journey. I like it though, I really do. It's different. It says 'I have a life outside of the office.' Which is great, because apparently you had nowhere else to go.

jokes.text: Hello, M. Sonny Crocket. I'm Tubbs.
jokes.setup: Ryan's new beard and business look
jokes.punchline: Michael comparing them to Miami Vice characters
jokes.explanation: Typical Michael pop culture reference. Shows his age and desperate attempt to be cool with outdated references.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what? You and Dwight look like you just stepped out of Miami Vice. You've got that whole thing going on — the stubble, the intensity. All you need is a Ferrari and a cocaine problem and you'd be Sonny Crockett.

jokes.text: Fire guy! Don't start any fires, Ryan. Fire guy! You weren't here for that. Here for what? When he started the fire.
jokes.setup: Michael calling Ryan 'fire guy'
jokes.punchline: Jim's confusion leading to explanation of Ryan's temp fire incident
jokes.explanation: Solid callback joke. The enthusiasm for referencing Ryan's past failures contrasts with his new power.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, why do you call him 'fire guy'?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Because he set a fire. In the office. He's a temp, and he set a fire.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It was actually pretty bad.

jokes.text: Look at you! You are so... mature and old and little man now. You're like our little man. Little old man boy. Bearded like man boy.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to compliment Ryan's maturity
jokes.punchline: Increasingly nonsensical and contradictory descriptors
jokes.explanation: Michael's verbal spiral shows his nervousness and inability to articulate. The contradictions (little man, old, boy) are endearingly confused.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Ryan, you're like a phoenix that's also a turtle. You move forward by going backward, but also sideways. You're a very mature... child-man. A man-child, but not in a bad way. You're like a wise baby. An old teenager. You're simultaneously the youngest old person I've ever met and the oldest young person.

jokes.text: I'd like your respect. I'm your boss now. You're gonna have to treat me the same way you treated Jan. So... That's a little kinky. I don't swing that way.
jokes.setup: Ryan demanding to be treated like Jan
jokes.punchline: Michael's immediate sexual interpretation and rejection
jokes.explanation: Michael's brain immediately going to sexual territory is perfectly in character. Good misdirection from professional to personal.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: I want to be treated like Jan.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's not gonna happen.

jokes.text: I think Ryan has a gay crush on me.
jokes.setup: Ryan trying to establish professional authority
jokes.punchline: Michael's complete misreading of the situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael delusion. His inability to recognize professional boundaries and tendency to personalize everything.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Ryan, I respect what you're doing here. You're trying to establish yourself as a leader. That's great. That's really great.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm gonna make you my assistant regional manager.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: I'm already the temp.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Exactly. You're ready for the next step. This is a big promotion.

jokes.text: Yeah, Ryan snapped at me. But there was this twinkle in his eye that I picked up on which said, 'Dude, we're friends. I'm doing this for appearances... And I love you.' His words.
jokes.setup: Michael in talking head explaining Ryan's harsh treatment
jokes.punchline: Elaborate fantasy conversation ending with 'His words' when Ryan said none of it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of Michael's delusion. The detailed fantasy conversation contrasted with 'His words' is perfectly executed self-deception.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm not going to let Ryan treat me like that. So I went home and I practiced what I was going to say to him the next day.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Ryan, you know what? I'm the Regional Manager here, and I think you owe me an apology.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: You're right, Michael. I'm sorry. You've been nothing but good to me.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know I have. And another thing—

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Michael, you're the best boss I've ever had. You're like a father to me.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And that's when he said all that stuff.

jokes.text: Wait a second. Last time I checked, Dunder-Mifflin already has a website. And quite frankly, I'm not really sure what's wrong with it.
jokes.setup: Ryan presenting new website initiative
jokes.punchline: Michael's defensive confusion about why they need a new website
jokes.explanation: Good representation of resistance to change. Michael's protective stance about status quo feels authentic to character.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Why do we need a new website? Our website is fine.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Michael, it's from 2003.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Exactly. It's a classic.

jokes.text: What if we don't wanna use a blackberry because they are stupid and pointless?
jokes.setup: Ryan explaining mandatory blackberry distribution
jokes.punchline: Dwight's blunt rejection with childish reasoning
jokes.explanation: Typical Dwight directness. His matter-of-fact dismissal of new technology fits his character's traditionalism.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Everyone will be required to carry a company BlackBerry.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: No. I don't like it. It's too small, and it hurts my hands.

jokes.text: You should call it Dunder-Mifflinfinity. You know, push the words together.
jokes.setup: Discussion of 'Dunder-Mifflin Infinity' name
jokes.punchline: Kevin's suggestion to smoosh the words together into an awkward portmanteau
jokes.explanation: Simple Kevin humor. His helpful but misguided suggestion feels authentic to his character's simple logic.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: What if we smoosh them together? Dunder-Mifflin-finity. No wait, Dunderfinity. Dunder... Miflin... Infinty. Dundermifflinfinty.

jokes.text: We're screwed. Who is? Us, you and me. The old timers. I am not old. You are old. You are, like, a 100. You're over 40. That's the cut off.
jokes.setup: Stanley warning about age discrimination
jokes.punchline: Michael's increasingly desperate denials about being old, ending with '40 is the cutoff'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Michael's vanity and fear of aging. The specific '40 cutoff' reveals his anxiety about his own age.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: You know what the real problem is? Age discrimination. I'm 62 years old, and nobody wants to hire me.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Stanley, that's not true. Age is just a number.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: A number that apparently matters a lot.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Look, I'm not old. I'm... I'm in my prime. I could run a marathon right now if I wanted to.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I have incredible stamina. People tell me I look like I'm in my thirties.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And even if I was old, which I'm not, 40 is the cutoff. Anything under 40, you're still young.

jokes.text: I'm telling you, this kid is the grim reaper. You deal with this, or you, me, Sammy, Phyllis, the chick you hit with the car, we're goners.
jokes.setup: Stanley explaining the threat Ryan poses
jokes.punchline: Casually including 'the chick you hit with the car' in list of vulnerable employees
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback delivery. Stanley's matter-of-fact inclusion of the car incident in a list of names is perfectly timed and casual.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Ryan is a temp. He's very dangerous. He could hurt Pam, he could hurt Erin, he could hurt the chick you hit with the car.

jokes.text: Dwight and Angela... dating. Have been for six months. Swear to god.
jokes.setup: Michael promising to reveal a brain-exploding secret
jokes.punchline: Information that's old news to Jim
jokes.explanation: Setup for the bigger joke that Jim already knows. Michael's excitement about sharing 'secret' information.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I have a secret that will blow your mind.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, what is it?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Pam and I... we've been secretly dating.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, we all knew that.

jokes.text: No, they have been dating for, like, two years. Since before your barbeque.
jokes.setup: Michael thinks he's revealing secret about Dwight and Angela
jokes.punchline: Jim not only knows but has known much longer than Michael
jokes.explanation: Perfect reversal of Michael's big reveal. Jim's superior knowledge deflates Michael's moment while showing Jim's observational skills.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know something about Dwight and Angela that nobody else knows.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh really? Because I've known about that for like two years.

jokes.text: You knew. And you didn't say anything? You didn't say anything to me. Fair enough.
jokes.setup: Michael accusing Jim of keeping secrets
jokes.punchline: Jim's point that Michael also kept the secret, leading to Michael's acceptance
jokes.explanation: Nice moment of Michael showing rare self-awareness and logical consistency when called out.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jim, you've been keeping secrets from me? That's not what we do here. We're a family.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, you knew about it the whole time. You kept the secret too.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh. Well... that's different. I was keeping it for a good reason.

jokes.text: But when a new client calls, you just have to randomly assign them to a sales person. You can't base who gets new clients on who you're sleeping with that week.
jokes.setup: Ryan addressing Pam about dating and work
jokes.punchline: The crude 'who you're sleeping with that week' comment implying promiscuity
jokes.explanation: Ryan's newfound arrogance and inappropriate workplace behavior. The cringe comes from his unprofessional personal attack.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: You can't date someone from work. It's unprofessional.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Says the guy who's slept with half the office.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: That's different. I'm in sales.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, and apparently you're selling yourself. Who are you sleeping with this week?

jokes.text: And... that is why we waited so long to tell people.
jokes.setup: Ryan's inappropriate comments about their relationship
jokes.punchline: Jim's understated response suggesting this reaction was predictable
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim response. His dry delivery suggests they anticipated exactly this kind of awkward workplace drama.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm not surprised. That's very Ryan.

jokes.text: You need to prepare yourself.
jokes.setup: Ryan discussing efficiency and changes
jokes.punchline: Vague but ominous warning delivered with cold authority
jokes.explanation: Ryan's transformation into corporate villain. The vague threat shows his new power-drunk personality.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Things are going to change around here. And not everyone is going to like it. But that's the price of progress.

jokes.text: We had a foreign exchange student live with us when I was young. And we called him my brother... And then he went home to what is now formerly Yugoslavia, taking all of my blue jeans with him. And I just spent the entire winter in shorts. That is what Ryan is like: a fake brother who steals your jeans.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining his feelings about Ryan through talking head
jokes.punchline: Elaborate analogy about foreign exchange student stealing jeans, applied to Ryan
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Michael analogy. The specific imagery of spending winter in shorts and the metaphor of a 'fake brother who steals your jeans' is perfectly absurd yet emotionally resonant.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know, it's like when you have a foreign exchange student living with you, and you really like them, you give them your best jeans, and then one day they just leave and take your jeans with them. That's what Ryan did to me.

jokes.text: I am dating a lot of guys. Good. A lot. Black guys, mostly. What?
jokes.setup: Kelly casually mentioning dating multiple people
jokes.punchline: Her oddly specific racial specification and Ryan's confused reaction
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable Kelly moment. The awkward oversharing and racial specification feels authentically cringe for her character.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: I'm dating three guys right now. I'm not like, exclusive or anything.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Yeah. There's Raj, who's a Pisces. There's Todd, who's also a Pisces. And there's this guy Kumar who's... I think a Gemini? But he's Indian, so it balances out the whole Pisces thing.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: That's not how any of this works.

jokes.text: How do you touch just one of these buttons at a time? I don't know. Did you even try?
jokes.setup: Older employees struggling with Blackberry technology
jokes.punchline: Stanley's blunt admission he didn't even attempt to use it
jokes.explanation: Good representation of generational tech divide. Stanley's honesty about not trying fits his lazy character.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: I've been trying to figure out this Blackberry all week.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: I didn't even try.

jokes.text: Six months ago, Karen Filippelli sent me an email asking me out. I said no because I was committed to our relationship. Well, I hope you're still committed, because I'm pregnant. And guess what buddy? I am keeping it.
jokes.setup: Ryan explaining his past faithfulness to Kelly
jokes.punchline: Kelly's dramatic pregnancy announcement as response
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from Ryan's example to Kelly's bombshell. The timing and Kelly's dramatic delivery make this a strong comedic moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: I've been faithful to you, Kelly. I've never cheated.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Well, I'm pregnant.

jokes.text: We have a date!
jokes.setup: Serious pregnancy discussion
jokes.punchline: Kelly's excited interpretation of dinner plans as a romantic date
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kelly reaction. Her ability to reframe serious situations as romantic opportunities is quintessential character behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Oh my God, are you taking me out to dinner? This is so romantic! We're having a baby and now you're sweeping me off my feet with a nice meal. I'm like, so in love with you right now.

jokes.text: Cauliflower and noodles. Baked potato on the side. I would prefer a public place.
jokes.setup: Dwight offering to cook romantic dinner
jokes.punchline: Angela's polite rejection preferring public dining
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Dwight's practical cooking and Angela's need for propriety even while dating him.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Angela, I would like to prepare an elaborate five-course meal for you this evening. I have already selected the wines and designed a menu around seasonal ingredients from my beet farm.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: That's very thoughtful, Dwight, but I'd prefer to go out to a restaurant instead.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A restaurant? But my cooking is exceptional. I have trained under the finest culinary minds in the tri-state area.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I'm sure you have, but I'd really feel more comfortable in a public setting.

jokes.text: So, elephant in the room, I have your old job. Well, not exactly... my job. A different title. Oh, well, excuse me. Same office. Same responsibilities. Different salary.
jokes.setup: Ryan addressing Jan about taking her position
jokes.punchline: Escalating list of similarities ending with salary difference implication
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Ryan's arrogance. The implication he makes more money than Jan adds insult to injury.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Jan, I know we're very similar. We both have ambition, we're both intelligent, we both make terrible decisions in our personal lives...

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Where is this going?

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: And we both deserve the same salary.

jokes.text: What is the actual deal with these things in terms of testicles? What? I don't wanna grow weird sperm in case we ever wanna have kids.
jokes.setup: Jim asking about Blackberry health effects
jokes.punchline: Specific concern about sperm health for future children
jokes.explanation: Awkward Jim moment showing relationship commitment through concern about future fertility. The bluntness is endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, is there any radiation from the BlackBerry that could affect... you know, sperm health? For future children?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I don't think so.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Because I want to have kids someday.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I know you do.

jokes.text: Creed? Yes, sir? Everything okay? Everything's cool, dude. I'm 30. Well, in november, I'll be 30.
jokes.setup: Ryan checking on employees after age discrimination speech
jokes.punchline: Creed claiming to be turning 30 (he's clearly much older)
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed absurdity. His obvious lie about his age delivered with complete confidence is quintessential character humor.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Hey Creed, how are you holding up after that age discrimination speech?

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Great! Actually, I'm turning 30 next month.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Creed, you're not turning 30.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Not with that attitude.

jokes.text: Did you know... that the age discrimination and employment act of 1967 prohibits employment discrimination based on age with respect to employees 40 years of age or older? I did.
jokes.setup: Michael reading legal text to challenge Ryan
jokes.punchline: Ryan's unimpressed 'I did' response
jokes.explanation: Michael's research deflated by Ryan's knowledge. Shows Michael's desperation versus Ryan's corporate training.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I did not have sexual relations with that woman.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: I did.

jokes.text: Because they're lame. No! Creed, no, they are not.
jokes.setup: Michael asking why society hates old people
jokes.punchline: Kevin's blunt 'Because they're lame' and Michael's defensive response
jokes.explanation: Kevin's honest brutality contrasted with Michael's desperate defense. Simple but effective character interaction.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Why does society hate old people?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Because they're lame.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's not... that's a fair point, actually.

jokes.text: In fact, many cultures revere old people because of their storytelling ability. Like the old lady from Titanic. Or the funny things that they can do, like, 'Where's the beef?'
jokes.setup: Michael defending elderly people's value
jokes.punchline: Examples of old lady from Titanic and ancient Wendy's commercial
jokes.explanation: Michael's cultural references showing his age and limited understanding. The examples undermine his point about respecting elders.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know, people say that old people are useless, but that's not true. I mean, look at the old lady from Titanic. She told that whole story for like three hours. That's leadership.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And there's that ancient Wendy's lady, 'Where's the beef?' She's still iconic. That's branding.

jokes.text: Who's this old fart? Did you just stagger off the street, out of a box or something? Who's this worthless bag of bones?
jokes.setup: Michael introduced Robert Dunder, company founder
jokes.punchline: Michael's deliberately insulting introduction to prove ageism point
jokes.explanation: Michael's misguided attempt at reverse psychology. His over-the-top insults are meant to shock but just create awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Robert Dunder, founder of Dunder Mifflin. Still kicking. Not dead yet. Probably will be soon though, right? I mean, look at him. He's ancient. But hey, that's not a reason to discriminate against someone in the workplace. Age is just a number. A really high number in his case.

jokes.text: Then I'll call David Wallace and you can explain to him why you threw the founder of the company out on his ancient butt.
jokes.setup: Ryan trying to end the meeting with Robert Dunder
jokes.punchline: Michael's threat referencing Dunder's 'ancient butt'
jokes.explanation: Michael's continued inappropriate language while trying to defend against ageism. The phrase 'ancient butt' undermines his own argument.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Hey, hey, hey! You can't leave! Robert, sit down. Sit down. I'm not done with you yet. You think you can just waltz into my office, into my conference room, and disrespect me? I will destroy you. I will go to the mat with you. I will take you down to the ground, and I will beat you senseless. I will rip off your arm and beat you with it. And then I'll use your ancient butt as a battering ram to knock down the walls of this building.

jokes.text: My grandma Schrute lived to be 101. My grandpa Mannheim is 103, and still puttering around down in Argentina. I tried to go visit him once but my travel visa was protested by the Shoah foundation.
jokes.setup: Dwight talking about his long-lived grandparents
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of Holocaust foundation blocking his travel to Argentina
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark Dwight humor. The casual implication about his grandfather's Nazi past delivered matter-of-factly is perfectly shocking and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My grandparents lived well into their 90s. My grandfather was particularly hardy. He could withstand extreme conditions.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I was planning a trip to Argentina, but it got blocked by the Holocaust Foundation. Apparently they have some concerns about my family history.

jokes.text: And then Mifflin, of course he killed himself later, but I knew Mifflin through the rotary club.
jokes.setup: Robert telling company history
jokes.punchline: Casually mentioning co-founder's suicide mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dark casual delivery. The matter-of-fact mention of suicide followed by mundane rotary club detail is expertly executed dark comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Robert Dunder
dialogue.line: We founded this company back in 1949. My partner and I had a vision to revolutionize the paper industry. He killed himself in '52, but anyway, we really took off after that.

jokes.text: Well, we have learned that you can't teach an old dog new tricks. Because it's illegal and you will go to jail.
jokes.setup: Michael concluding his ageism presentation
jokes.punchline: Taking idiom literally and applying legal consequences
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay showing Michael's twisted logic. Taking the idiom literally and adding legal threat is good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And that concludes my presentation on ageism in the workplace. Now, I know some of you may have heard the phrase 'break a leg' before. Well, if you actually break someone's leg, that's assault. You will go to jail. So don't do that.

jokes.text: You know what? You're right. I'm feeling kind of nauseous, anyway. So... we can just skip it.
jokes.setup: Ryan suggesting expensive restaurant
jokes.punchline: Kelly's sudden nausea as excuse to avoid dinner
jokes.explanation: Kelly's manipulation showing she wasn't really pregnant. The fake nausea is a weak attempt at maintaining the pregnancy lie.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: I was thinking we could go to that new French restaurant downtown. It's supposed to be amazing.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Oh my god, yes! I love French food. Wait... actually, I'm feeling really nauseous. I think I'm gonna be sick.

jokes.text: Are you enjoying your vegetarian noodles? Very much. How is your meat? Dry, delicious.
jokes.setup: Angela and Dwight on awkward dinner date
jokes.punchline: Stilted conversation with Dwight's contradictory 'dry, delicious' description
jokes.explanation: Good cringe dinner scene. The formal awkwardness and Dwight's contradictory food description capture their relationship dysfunction.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: So, Angela, I've prepared a seven-course meal. Each course is dry, yet delicious.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Dry? That doesn't sound appetizing.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Well, that's because you lack refinement. Dry is a delicacy among those with sophisticated palates.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I have a sophisticated palate, Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Then you'll appreciate the subtle notes of desiccation in this braised beet. Dry, yet delicious.

jokes.text: I heard a joke today. Oh, that's funny. Yes, it was.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to make conversation
jokes.punchline: Angela's misunderstanding leading to Dwight's literal confirmation
jokes.explanation: Perfect miscommunication comedy. Angela's premature response and Dwight's literal confirmation of the joke being funny creates awkward loop.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Angela, I was wondering if you'd like to go out for dinner sometime. You know, as friends.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Dwight, are you asking me on a date?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: No, no. I said as friends. Two friends, eating food together in a restaurant.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: So you're asking me to go out with you.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Yes. But as friends.

jokes.text: Every time I look in your eyes, I see Sprinkles' stiff, lifeless body. Then don't look in my eyes. Look right here. It's an old sales trick.
jokes.setup: Angela explaining why she can't be with Dwight
jokes.punchline: Dwight's practical solution using sales technique
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Dwight practicality. His sales-technique solution to romantic trauma is perfectly in character and absurdly logical.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I can't be with you, Dwight. We're just too different. You're obsessed with beets and martial arts, and I have standards.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Angela, you're using classic objection handling. Let me reframe this. Beets are a renewable resource, much like your capacity for love. And martial arts teaches discipline, which I apply to our relationship. By closing this sale, we both win.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: That's not how this works.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: It always works. ABC: Always Be Closing.

jokes.text: I will leave your toothbrush on top of your tire tomorrow morning.
jokes.setup: Angela breaking up with Dwight
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific location for returning his toothbrush
jokes.explanation: Weird Angela specificity. The tire location for toothbrush return is oddly practical yet bizarre, fitting both characters.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I'm breaking up with you, Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What? Why?

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: It's not working anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: We can work through this.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I'm leaving your toothbrush on the desk by the copy machine.

jokes.text: Ryan wants everything in our company to be about emails and I.M.S. But I think he is forgetting about the original instant message. Letters attached to baskets of food.
jokes.setup: Michael criticizing Ryan's technology focus
jokes.punchline: Calling gift basket notes 'the original instant message'
jokes.explanation: Clever Michael logic connecting old-fashioned notes to modern technology. The 'original instant message' concept is genuinely witty.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what? Gift basket notes are the original instant message. You write it, you put it in a basket, boom, instant communication.

jokes.text: I don't understand what the big deal is. You lied about being pregnant. Right, so? You really don't understand why that makes me angry? No.
jokes.setup: Ryan confronting Kelly about pregnancy lie
jokes.punchline: Kelly's genuine confusion about why lying about pregnancy is wrong
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kelly obliviousness. Her genuine confusion about the moral problem with her lie shows complete self-absorption.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: You lied about being pregnant? What is wrong with you?

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: What? Why is that wrong? I was just trying to make you jealous so you'd pay attention to me.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Because you can't just lie about being pregnant!

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Why not? Lots of people do it.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: No they don't!

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Well I don't know, Ryan. I'm not a scientist.

jokes.text: All right, I just have to ask. Now that we're public... is the magic gone? It's funny you bring that up, because yes, it is. I knew it. I now find you repulsive.
jokes.setup: Kevin asking if public relationship killed the romance
jokes.punchline: Jim and Pam's fake escalation to 'repulsive'
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection comedy. Jim and Pam's fake dramatic escalation plays perfectly with Kevin's expectations and their actual happiness.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Did going public kill the romance?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, no, we still have plenty of romance.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, we do things.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We hold hands...

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: We kiss...

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Sometimes we do both at the same time.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: It's repulsive.

jokes.text: Ever since I was a kid, people have been telling me that I can't do things. 'You can't be on the team.' 'You can't move on to second grade.' Well, now they're telling me that I can't win back clients using old fashioned business methods... And FYI, I eventually hated second grade. And I was the biggest kid in class.
jokes.setup: Michael's inspirational speech about overcoming obstacles
jokes.punchline: Admitting he hated second grade and was biggest because he was held back
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Michael speech that undermines itself. The revelation about repeating second grade and being biggest due to age perfectly deflates his inspiration.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know, I've always believed that obstacles are just opportunities in disguise. I was the biggest kid in second grade, and everyone thought it was because I was so strong, so athletic. But the truth is, I was held back. I hated second grade. Hated it. But you know what? That made me who I am today.

jokes.text: What about cash? Cash can buy whatever you want, including a gift basket, so... it's kind of the best gift ever. What about a gift basket full of cash? Yes! Cash basket!
jokes.setup: Debate about whether gift baskets are the ultimate gift
jokes.punchline: Dwight's suggestion of cash-filled gift basket combining both concepts
jokes.explanation: Good logical progression from Stanley's point to Dwight's solution. The 'cash basket' concept is absurd but logical.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: A gift basket is the ultimate gift. It's thoughtful, it's generous, and it says 'I care.'

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: That's ridiculous. Cash is the ultimate gift. You can buy whatever you want.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: You're both wrong. The ultimate gift is a gift basket... filled with cash.

jokes.text: Hey, brah. Been meaning to ask you, can we get some Red Bull for these things? Sometimes a guy's gotta ride the bull. Am I right? Later, skater.
jokes.setup: Ryan's new corporate lifestyle
jokes.punchline: Trying too hard to be cool with 'brah', 'ride the bull', 'later skater'
jokes.explanation: Good cringe Ryan moment. His desperate attempt at cool corporate bro speak shows his insecurity and transformation.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Brah, you gotta ride the bull, later skater.

jokes.text: Why do you use that thing? It lets them know where you are at all times. Who? The government, spy satellites, private detectives. Ex-girlfriends.
jokes.setup: Michael asking about GPS usage
jokes.punchline: Dwight's paranoid list ending with mundane 'ex-girlfriends'
jokes.explanation: Good Dwight paranoia escalating to personal concerns. The jump from government surveillance to ex-girlfriends is perfectly Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight, why won't you use the GPS in your car?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: GPS is a government tracking device. They can see everywhere I go. I've disabled mine.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight, no one cares where you go.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's what you think. The government, the NSA, the CIA, the KGB, my ex-girlfriends...

jokes.text: Next night, I'm out at a bar, 2:00 AM. I figure I'll get a sandwich. 'Cause you can get a sandwich any time of the night. I run into Vince Vaughn. No way. Literally. Dude, you are so money. But you dot even know it. But you do.
jokes.setup: Ryan telling story about celebrity encounter
jokes.punchline: Quoting Swingers movie while claiming to have met Vince Vaughn
jokes.explanation: Ryan's obvious fabrication using movie quotes. Shows his desperate need to seem cool and connected to celebrity culture.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: So I'm at this party, right? And Vince Vaughn walks up to me. Just walks right up.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: And I'm like, 'You're Vince Vaughn.' And he goes, 'Yeah.' So I quote Swingers back at him: 'You're so money and you don't even know it.'

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: He just stared at me. Didn't laugh or anything.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's because he didn't write Swingers, Ryan. Jon Favreau wrote and starred in it.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Well, Vince Vaughn was in it.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you basically just quoted another guy's movie to the guy who was in the movie. That's not impressive.

jokes.text: He has a killer job. He's rich. He smells like what I think Pierce Brosnan probably smells like. He wears really cool rich guy cloth. And he can get any girl that he wants.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining why Ryan is awesome
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and weird compliments ending with 'rich guy cloth'
jokes.explanation: Good Andy hero worship. The specific Pierce Brosnan smell reference and 'rich guy cloth' show his shallow material focus.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Ryan is awesome because he's got this energy, you know? And he's so smart. Like, really smart. But not in an annoying way. He's got great hair. Like, really great hair. The kind of hair that just catches the light perfectly. And his shirts are made of this really nice fabric. Like, rich guy cloth.

jokes.text: That guy was so st...
jokes.setup: Michael starting to insult difficult client
jokes.punchline: Cut off mid-word, presumably about to say 'stupid'
jokes.explanation: Good editing beat showing Michael's frustration. The self-censorship suggests some awareness of professionalism.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Listen here, you stupid—

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: [cuts him off]

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm gonna do some mock-ups and then turn those into thumbnails. Maybe do some, uh, splash frames. I don't know what I'm talking about, but I'm excited.
jokes.setup: Pam talking about graphic design work
jokes.punchline: Admitting she doesn't know the terminology she's using
jokes.explanation: Sweet Pam moment showing honest enthusiasm despite inexperience. Her self-awareness makes the fake expertise endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I'm really getting into graphic design. I've been studying composition, kerning, negative space... I don't actually know what any of those words mean, but they sound really professional when I say them.

jokes.text: Look... We want you back. Can you offer lower prices? Well, no. Then we're not coming back. He's not coming back. It's over. No, it's not. No, he's right. Accept it, why would he come back?
jokes.setup: Michael trying to win back client
jokes.punchline: Dwight accidentally supporting the client's rejection
jokes.explanation: Good Dwight undermining Michael. His logical agreement with the client's position sabotages Michael's sales pitch.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Come on, just give us another chance. We can do better.

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.line: I don't know, Michael. You guys really let us down.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: With all due respect, they're right. Your service was subpar at best.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Being honest, sir. It's what you always say we should be.

jokes.text: Maybe it's a short cut. It said, go to the right. It can't mean that. It's a lake there! The machine knows. Stop yelling at me! No, it's up there! There's no road here!
jokes.setup: GPS giving directions into lake
jokes.punchline: Michael's blind faith in technology leading to driving into water
jokes.explanation: Classic Office physical comedy setup. Michael's stubborn faith in technology over common sense creates perfect disaster scenario.

dialogue.speaker: GPS
dialogue.line: Turn right ahead.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, GPS says turn right.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Michael, that's a lake.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: The GPS knows what it's doing, Dwight. I trust it.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's never steered me wrong before.

jokes.text: Remain calm. I have trained for this. Okay. Exit the window! Here we go. Make a U-turn. If possible. Look out for nature!
jokes.setup: Car in lake emergency
jokes.punchline: Dwight's survival training contrasted with continued GPS directions and Michael's 'nature' warning
jokes.explanation: Perfect chaos comedy. Dwight's preparedness, ongoing GPS instructions, and Michael's fear of nature create layered comedy gold.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Don't go in the water! I'm warning you, nature is unpredictable!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Michael, I have extensive survival training. I can handle this.

dialogue.speaker: GPS
dialogue.line: In 500 feet, turn right.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'm going in.

dialogue.speaker: GPS
dialogue.line: In 50 feet, turn right into the lake.

jokes.text: Do you wanna go out to dinner tonight? Oh, is it... Wear something nice. No. What? I just... wanted to have dinner. I'm sorry. I'm dating Jim.
jokes.setup: Ryan asking Pam to dinner professionally
jokes.punchline: His 'wear something nice' making it clearly romantic, leading to rejection
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Ryan's arrogance and inappropriate workplace behavior. The 'wear something nice' reveals his true intentions.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: So I was thinking, maybe we could grab dinner sometime? You know, to discuss... business stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh. Um, sure, that sounds nice.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Great. And, uh, wear something nice.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh. Oh no. Ryan, I can't. I'm with Jim.

jokes.text: I guess he can't get any girl he wants.
jokes.setup: Jim witnessing Ryan's rejection by Pam
jokes.punchline: Direct callback to Andy's earlier claim about Ryan
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback execution. Jim's quiet satisfaction and direct reference to Andy's earlier hero worship is expertly timed.

jokes.text: Can you call us a cab, please? I'm gonna-- Oh, sorry! Look. My clothes are so wet. Nice leather. My shoes are so muddy! All right, here you go. Take it back!
jokes.setup: Michael and Dwight returning soaking wet to demand gift basket back
jokes.punchline: Michael's passive-aggressive dripping on furniture until client gives in
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy payoff. Michael's petty revenge through wetness and the client's eventual surrender creates satisfying resolution.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We're not leaving until we get that gift basket back.

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, but that's not going to happen.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh, okay. Well then, I guess we'll just have to stay here and drip.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's right. Drip all over your furniture.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yeah. This couch? Very absorbent. We could be here for hours.

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.line: Fine! Fine, take the basket!

jokes.text: Where are the turtles?! Where are they? I ate them, okay? I ate the turtles. They're gone!
jokes.setup: Michael discovering missing chocolate turtles from gift basket
jokes.punchline: Client's desperate admission under interrogation pressure
jokes.explanation: Good absurd escalation. Michael's intense turtle interrogation and the client's confession creates ridiculous but satisfying conclusion.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Where are the chocolate turtles? They were a gift for our most important client.

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.line: I... I don't know what you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Really? Because according to my notes, you were the last person to see them. And frankly, your story doesn't add up.

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.line: Okay, fine! I ate them! I ate all of them! I couldn't help myself! They were just sitting there, looking at me with their little turtle faces!

jokes.text: I just drove my car into a lake. You did what? I drove my car... into a f*ing lake. Why, you may ask, did do this? Well... because of a machine. A machine told me to drive into a lake. And I did it. I did it because I trusted Ryan's precious technology.
jokes.setup: Michael announcing his anti-technology stance
jokes.punchline: Dramatic revelation about driving into lake because GPS told him to
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Michael speech. His dramatic presentation of the lake incident as evidence against technology is perfectly absurd and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I don't trust technology. It's unreliable and dangerous.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Last week my GPS told me to drive into a lake. I did it.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Drove right in. Had to be towed out by a farmer with a tractor.

jokes.text: Game, set, match. Point. Scott. Game over. End of game.
jokes.setup: Michael concluding his anti-technology argument
jokes.punchline: Mixing tennis and general game terminology with his own name
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael victory celebration. His confused mixing of game terminology and self-aggrandizement is quintessential character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm not anti-technology. I'm pro-Michael. And Michael doesn't need a smartphone to be smart. Michael's already got game. In fact, Michael's got such game, I could play tennis with a blackberry and still win. Love-love, Michael Scott.

jokes.text: People will never be replaced by machines. In the end, life and business are about human connections. And computers are about trying to murder you in a lake.
jokes.setup: Michael's philosophical conclusion about technology
jokes.punchline: Dramatic claim that computers try to murder you in lakes
jokes.explanation: Absolute Michael gold. The profound-sounding setup contrasted with 'computers trying to murder you in a lake' is perfectly executed absurdist philosophy.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about technology a lot lately. And I've come to a conclusion.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Computers are trying to murder you in lakes.

tmdb_overview: Ryan returns to Scranton with a plan to bring Dunder Mifflin into the digital age by launching a new website - Dunder Mifflin Infinity. Michael is worried about ageism and attempts to win back clients using old fashion business skills. Relationships are on the forefront as Ryan returns to an eager Kelly, Jim and Pam are outed to the office, and Angela is still upset with Dwight about her cat.
insight: Online sales panic spawns 79 jokes, but escalation-heavy structure dilutes impact to 75.9.
